steps.title: Identify the definitional challenge Maddux raises
steps.content:
Maddux's central point is that there is no objective definition of what constitutes productivity.

He demonstrates this through an example: the same activity—reading a book—can be viewed as productive or idle depending on who is doing it and what matters to them.

Sharif concludes that life satisfaction drops when people spend free time unproductively, citing watching TV and playing games as examples, but this conclusion assumes that “unproductive” is a category everyone agrees on.

steps.title: Match the response to Maddux's objection
steps.content:
C correctly identifies the flaw in Sharif's reasoning: she assumes TV and games are universally unproductive, but Maddux has shown there is no universal definition of what counts as productive.

This is precisely what Maddux would caution against—an overly broad assumption that categories like “unproductive” have the same meaning for everyone.

The other options either accept Sharif's framework without challenge, misidentify what Maddux contests, or overstate his position.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
